title: I Built an Interview Tool That Deliberately Does Less Than Every Competitor. Here's Why That Works.
author: GrifeDev
summary:
There’s a feature war happening in the AI interview copilot space.

Every tool is racing to do more: always-on listening, invisible overlays, “stealth mode,” auto-detection of questions, real-time screen analysis running constantly in the background.

I went the other direction. I built a tool that waits until you press a button.

And after 14 months and 500 users, I’m convinced the “less” approach produces better results than the “more” approach. Not philosophically - technically.

Let me explain.

Every major interview copilot (Final Round AI, LockedIn AI, Sensei AI, ParakeetAI) works roughly the same way: the moment your call starts, it begins capturing audio, feeding it through speech-to-text, and generating AI responses continuously.

This sounds better. More data should mean better understanding, right?

When you feed Whisper (or any speech-to-text
full_text:
There’s a feature war happening in the AI interview copilot space.

Every tool is racing to do more: always-on listening, invisible overlays, “stealth mode,” auto-detection of questions, real-time screen analysis running constantly in the background.

I went the other direction. I built a tool that waits until you press a button.

And after 14 months and 500 users, I’m convinced the “less” approach produces better results than the “more” approach. Not philosophically - technically.

Let me explain.

The problem with always-on transcription

Every major interview copilot (Final Round AI, LockedIn AI, Sensei AI, ParakeetAI) works roughly the same way: the moment your call starts, it begins capturing audio, feeding it through speech-to-text, and generating AI responses continuously.

This sounds better. More data should mean better understanding, right?

In practice, it doesn’t.

When you feed Whisper (or any speech-to-text model) a 45-minute audio stream, it processes everything: the “hey, can you hear me okay?”, the interviewer’s tangent about their weekend, the notification sounds from your Slack, the background noise from whatever coffee shop someone’s sitting in. Each of these introduces small transcription errors. Individually, they’re minor. Compounded over 45 minutes, they cascade.

The downstream effect: GPT-4 receives a transcription riddled with artifacts. It starts referencing context that was never part of the actual question. The AI response drifts. You glance at it during the interview and it’s talking about something the interviewer didn’t ask.

I’ve seen this firsthand. I tested three competitor tools before building mine. In a mock interview, one tool transcribed “walk me through your approach to caching” as “walk me through your approach to catching” - and generated a response about error handling. In a real interview, that’s worse than no help at all.

What happens with focused recording

VoiceMeetAI works differently. You listen to the interviewer’s question naturally. When they’re done (or while they’re asking), you press Record. When the question is complete, you press Stop.

The tool now has 15-60 seconds of clean, focused audio. One speaker, one question, minimal background noise.

Whisper transcribes this accurately because the signal-to-noise ratio is dramatically better. GPT-4 receives a clean input and generates a response that’s actually about the question that was asked.

The trade-off is obvious: there’s a small delay. You have to press a button. It’s not automatic. But the output quality is meaningfully better.

I ran informal tests across 20 mock interviews - 10 with always-on recording, 10 with manual recording of just the question. Manual recording produced relevant, usable AI responses about 85% of the time. Always-on produced relevant responses about 60% of the time. The other 40% were either off-topic, referenced phantom context, or arrived too late to be useful.

The anxiety problem nobody talks about

Here’s something I didn’t expect when I started building this.

Early users who’d previously tried always-on tools told me something consistent: the always-listening model made them more anxious, not less.

Think about it. You’re already nervous in an interview. Now add a layer of awareness: “Is the AI picking up the right context? Is it generating something useful? Should I look at it? Is the interviewer noticing me looking at it?” The tool that’s supposed to reduce cognitive load is actually adding to it.

With manual control, the dynamic shifts. You’re listening to the question like a normal human. When you want help, you actively choose to get it. When you don’t, the tool is invisible. You control the interaction, not the other way around.

Multiple users described it as the difference between “having a copilot” and “having someone watching over your shoulder.”

The market is going the wrong direction

I pay close attention to competitors. Here’s what I see:

Every funded company in this space is racing toward “undetectable.” LockedIn AI markets a “True Stealth Mode.” ParakeetAI’s entire value proposition is being invisible. Interview Coder (now Cluely) literally branded itself as an undetectable cheating tool - and then suffered a data breach exposing 83,000 users and a revenue fraud scandal.

Meanwhile, Final Round AI - the market leader with 10M+ claimed users - has a 17% complaint rate on Trustpilot. The complaints are almost all about billing: auto-renewal countdown timers, confusing credit systems, charges that appear without clear consent. Their customer support takes 5-7 days to respond.

LockedIn AI got caught using the same actor across 14+ fake testimonial videos.

The trust level in this entire category is collapsing. And every company’s response is to double down on “stealth” and “undetectable” - features that, by the way, don’t actually work. Chrome extensions are visible during screen share. Tab switching triggers detection systems. Eye-tracking on proctored interviews catches gaze patterns. The “undetectable” promise is a lie being sold to desperate people.

I decided to build in the opposite direction. Not because I’m more ethical than anyone (I’m a solo dev, not a saint), but because the “less is more” approach actually produces a better tool. Manual recording, clean transcription, relevant AI responses, transparent about what it does. No stealth mode. No “invisible overlay.” No $148/month pricing.

The screenshot feature that surprised me

I added screenshot capture almost as an afterthought. The idea: if the interviewer shares their screen with a coding task, system design diagram, or error message, the user can screenshot it and get an AI analysis.

It became one of the most-used features.

System design interviews are the clearest use case. The interviewer shares a complex architecture diagram - boxes, arrows, service names, data flows - and says “walk me through how you’d improve this.” You have maybe 15 seconds to absorb something you’ve never seen before while looking composed on camera.

With screenshot capture, you grab the diagram and get an instant breakdown: what the services are, how they connect, where the likely bottlenecks exist. Instead of “uh, let me look at this for a moment,” you open with “the main bottleneck I see is between the API gateway and the cache layer, and here’s why…”

It also works for coding prompts pasted into shared docs, error outputs during debugging rounds, and any visual information shared on screen. The common thread: interviews increasingly involve visual prompts that you need to process and respond to in real time.

What I’d tell other solo devs building in this space

I’ve been at this for 14 months. 500 users. 4.5 stars on the Chrome Web Store. Under $500/month in revenue. No funding, no employees, no marketing budget.

Here’s what I’ve learned:

The product was never the bottleneck. Distribution was. I spent 80% of my time building features and 20% finding users. That ratio should have been inverted the moment the core product was stable. If you’re a solo dev reading this - flip your time allocation earlier than you think you should.

Constraints are features. I couldn’t afford the compute for always-on recording. So I made it manual. The constraint produced a technically superior product. When you can’t match a funded competitor feature-for-feature, look for the constraint that becomes a strength.

Don’t chase “undetectable.” The market is shifting. Companies are adding detection. Users are getting burned by tools that promise invisibility and can’t deliver. Build something that works well and is honest about what it does. That’s a more sustainable position than “we swear the interviewer can’t see it.”

The interview system is the real problem. LeetCode grinding, artificial time pressure, memorizing solutions you’ll never use at work - the process is broken and everyone knows it. But until it changes, people need tools to navigate it. Building for that reality isn’t cheating. It’s pragmatism.

Try it

VoiceMeetAI is a Chrome extension. Works on Google Meet, Zoom, Microsoft Teams - anything running in a browser tab.

Press Record -> get transcription -> get structured AI response. Screenshot capture for visual prompts. Manual control, not always-listening.

If you’re in interview season and the problem isn’t your knowledge but your ability to communicate it under pressure, this might help.

https://voicemeetai.com/
